RTIGER reimplementation benchmark: nilHMM C++/Rcpp vs Julia

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

Supplement (optimization): correctness-first equivalence, then throughput/memory at the operating rigidity r=250

Author

Fausto Rodríguez-Zapata

Published

July 20, 2026

TipMeasured on real Arabidopsis data, at the operating rigidity r=250

Numbers, tables and figures below are the live benchmark at this dataset’s operating rigidity r = 250 (scripts/bench_rtiger_r250.R for the C++ core, scripts/rtiger_julia_conv_worker.jl for the original), not placeholders. The both cores are measured at all five panel sizes (the original’s full 110k fit takes ~43 min, vs ~0.2 s for the C++/Rcpp core).

Thesis. nilHMM reimplements the RTIGER rigid-HMM core in C++/Rcpp (nilhmm/src/rtiger.cpp), removing the Julia backend that upstream RTIGER [Campos-Martin 2023] requires through JuliaCall. The reimplementation is a prerequisite, not a contribution: the paper’s simulation-scale calibration and benchmarking (a caller rerun across many parameter settings on 104-105-marker panels) are only tractable on a laptop once the per-fit cost and the Julia runtime dependency are gone. This note establishes two things, in order of importance:

  1. Correctness first. The C++/Rcpp core returns the same answer as the Julia RTIGER, Viterbi-identical ancestry paths and fitted parameters agreeing to float-summation tolerance, from identical initialization on real data. Result: 0 mismatches at every size including the full 109,703-marker panel (329,109 of 329,109 states), same iteration count, params to <=4e-6.
  2. Then speed/memory. Only after equivalence is established do we report the throughput and memory gains. Result: the C++ core is near-linear (per-iter markers^0.98) vs the original’s markers^1.69 at r=250; full-fit speed-up ~2,000x at the smallest size to ~12,000x at the full 110k panel, and ~6-7x less resident memory.
NoteRigidity is collapsed, so r=250 is nearly free for the C++ core

RTIGER’s rigid (Erlang-r) segment prior is implemented in the C++ core in collapsed form, a windowed emission product plus an r-step transition offset, not an explicit s x r dwell-state enumeration (verified in rtiger.cpp, a bit-identical port of the Julia rHMM_methods.jl). Consequently the C++ per-iteration time and memory are essentially independent of r, whereas the upstream original recomputes each length-r emission window at O(T s r). At the operating r=250 that makes the windowed-product optimization a first-order win, on top of the emission M-step regrouping and the streaming that removes the sample-count (N) memory factor. See the paper supplement (nilhmm-paper/supplement_optimization.tex, Sec. 1) for the per-step complexity accounting.

NoteCompute is separated from this note

The C++ benchmark is run by scripts/bench_rtiger_r250.R: it builds the shared Arabidopsis Col x Ler panel, thins it by odd index to five sizes, fits the nilHMM C++ core at each size (to convergence + a per-iteration delta trace), and compares against the original-Julia oracle. The original at r=250 is run at all five sizes by scripts/rtiger_julia_conv_worker.jl (per-iteration log + params/Viterbi dump). Peak memory is measured on cleanly thinned inputs: scripts/materialize_thinned_panel.R writes thin_L{0..4}/, then scripts/bench_rtiger_cpp_memory_markers.R (C++, RTIGER_RIG=250) and scripts/rtiger_julia_mem_worker.jl (original) read only that thinned set. Figures are written by scripts/rtiger_r250_plot.R to nilhmm-paper/figures/; this note only loads and displays the CSVs and figures. The C++ sweep runs in seconds; the original’s full panel takes ~43 min.

Inputs staged from the fork (see DATA.md): the 109,703-locus shared-3 panel (data/rtiger_shared3_input/), originally from BioProject PRJNA720439 / SRP313911.

Machine/toolchain for the timings is recorded per run in results/bench/rtiger_env.txt (Apple M4, 10 cores, 24 GB, macOS 26.5; nilHMM C++/Rcpp built native arm64 with Apple clang 17 at -O2; R 4.5.2; single-threaded; original-Julia baseline on Julia 1.12.6, same hardware). The core’s forward/backward, Viterbi, and emission M-step are hand-written scalar C++ (LinkingTo: only Rcpp and RcppParallel; no Armadillo, Eigen, or external BLAS/LAPACK), so the system linear-algebra library cannot affect these timings.

Show code 
suppressMessages({
  library(data.table)
})
BENCH <- here::here("results/bench")
f <- function(x) file.path(BENCH, x)

1. Why reimplement in C++/Rcpp

Two properties of the upstream implementation motivate the port:

  • Runtime dependency. Upstream RTIGER runs its time-critical core in Julia via JuliaCall; a matching Julia + package environment must be installed and loaded. For an R package intended to run many callers on many parameter settings, that cross-runtime dependency is both a friction and a reproducibility hazard.
  • Per-fit cost at density. The paper works at 104-105 markers per line and reruns callers across calibration sweeps. The fit must be cheap enough to loop.

The C++/Rcpp core keeps the rigid-HMM arithmetic (rHMM EM + rigid Viterbi + segment boundary postprocessing) but runs in-process with no external runtime.

2. Equivalence (correctness first)

The claim the port must earn: same answer as Julia RTIGER. Both cores are run to full convergence from identical deterministic initialization at r=250, at all five sizes including the full 109,703-marker panel, and outputs compared position-by-position.

Show code 
eq <- fread(f("rtiger_equiv_r250.csv"))
knitr::kable(
  eq,
  col.names = c(
    "level", "markers/sample", "iters (C++)", "iters (Julia)",
    "Viterbi total", "mismatches", "param max-diff"
  ),
  caption = "nilHMM C++/Rcpp vs upstream-original Julia RTIGER at r=250, run to convergence from the identical deterministic init, at all five sizes including the full 109,703-marker panel: same EM iteration count, Viterbi-identical ancestry paths (0 mismatches), parameters agreeing to float-summation tolerance."
)
nilHMM C++/Rcpp vs upstream-original Julia RTIGER at r=250, run to convergence from the identical deterministic init, at all five sizes including the full 109,703-marker panel: same EM iteration count, Viterbi-identical ancestry paths (0 mismatches), parameters agreeing to float-summation tolerance.
level markers/sample iters (C++) iters (Julia) Viterbi total mismatches param max-diff
0 109703 4 4 329109 0 3.7e-06
1 54852 3 3 164556 0 3.0e-07
2 27426 4 4 82278 0 3.1e-06
3 13713 3 3 41139 0 6.0e-07
4 6857 3 3 20571 0 4.0e-07

The two cores descend the identical path to convergence at the full panel (right); the left panel is the speed contrast:

3. Throughput, scaling with markers

Shared-panel head-to-head on the five thinned sizes (6,857 -> 13,713 -> 27,426 -> 54,852 -> 109,703), all measured for both cores. The figure plots per-iteration wall time, whose log-log slope is the empirical complexity exponent: at r=250 the original is markers^1.69, the C++/Rcpp core markers^0.98 (linear). The table reports full-fit wall time to convergence and the resulting speed-up.

Show code 
sc <- fread(f("rtiger_marker_scaling_r250.csv"))
w <- dcast(sc, mps ~ core, value.var = "total")[order(-mps)]
w[, speedup := round(orig / cpp)]
knitr::kable(
  w[, .(
    `markers/sample` = mps, `C++/Rcpp (s)` = round(cpp, 3),
    `orig Julia (s)` = round(orig), `speed-up` = speedup
  )],
  caption = "Full-fit wall time to convergence at r=250 (3 samples, eps=0.01), all five sizes measured; the speed-up grows with problem size because the C++ core is near-linear."
)
Full-fit wall time to convergence at r=250 (3 samples, eps=0.01), all five sizes measured; the speed-up grows with problem size because the C++ core is near-linear.
markers/sample C++/Rcpp (s) orig Julia (s) speed-up
109703 0.205 2551 12444
54852 0.078 948 12150
27426 0.053 305 5756
13713 0.020 74 3680
6857 0.010 20 1973

4. Memory

The C++ E-step folds each chain into fixed-size pooled sufficient statistics (the s x s transition counts, the start counts, and a map of gamma-weighted counts over the distinct (k, n) read-count pairs) and discards each chain’s per-position zeta/gamma/alpha/beta arrays immediately. Peak scratch is therefore one chain’s worth, O(T_max s^2), independent of the sample count N, whereas the array-retaining original holds all chains at once, O(N T s^2). Because the rigidity is collapsed, the C++ memory is also essentially independent of r.

Show code 
cm <- fread(f("rtiger_memory_markers_r250.csv"))
jm <- fread(f("rtiger_julia_memory_markers_r250.csv"))
setnames(cm, "peak_rss_mib", "C++/Rcpp (MiB)")
setnames(jm, "peak_rss_mib", "orig Julia (MiB)")
mm <- merge(cm, jm, by = "markers", all = TRUE)[order(-markers)]
mm[, ratio := round(`orig Julia (MiB)` / `C++/Rcpp (MiB)`, 1)]
knitr::kable(mm,
  col.names = c("markers/sample", "C++/Rcpp (MiB)", "orig Julia (MiB)", "ratio"),
  caption = "Peak resident memory at r=250 (3 samples), C++ vs original Julia, all five sizes measured on cleanly thinned inputs (each process loads only its own marker set). Both scale with marker count (per-chain arrays are O(T)); the ~6-7x separation is the original's per-chain array retention, and it widens further on the sample axis."
)
Peak resident memory at r=250 (3 samples), C++ vs original Julia, all five sizes measured on cleanly thinned inputs (each process loads only its own marker set). Both scale with marker count (per-chain arrays are O(T)); the ~6-7x separation is the original’s per-chain array retention, and it widens further on the sample axis.
markers/sample C++/Rcpp (MiB) orig Julia (MiB) ratio
109703 167.0 982.3 5.9
54852 138.9 823.1 5.9
27426 114.6 806.1 7.0
13713 97.0 674.8 7.0
6857 93.1 619.1 6.6

On cleanly thinned inputs both cores scale with marker count (the per-chain arrays are O(T)); the ~6-7x separation is the original’s per-chain array retention. The asymptotically meaningful axis is the sample count N, on which the streamed C++ scratch does not grow while the original’s does.

5. Reproducing

scripts/bench_rtiger_r250.R builds the shared panel from the staged Col x Ler inputs, runs the C++ core at each size (to convergence + delta trace), compares against the original-Julia oracle at all five sizes, and writes the CSVs. scripts/rtiger_julia_conv_worker.jl <orig_base> <level> 250 produces the original runs; scripts/rtiger_r250_plot.R writes the figures. Only the built nilHMM C++ core and (for the small original sizes) Julia are needed.

::: {.callout-tip title="Measured on real Arabidopsis data, at the operating rigidity r=250"}
Numbers, tables and figures below are the live benchmark at this dataset's operating
rigidity **r = 250** (`scripts/bench_rtiger_r250.R` for the C++ core,
`scripts/rtiger_julia_conv_worker.jl` for the original), not placeholders. The
both cores are measured at all five panel sizes (the original's full 110k fit takes
~43 min, vs ~0.2 s for the C++/Rcpp core).
:::

**Thesis.** nilHMM reimplements the RTIGER rigid-HMM core in **C++/Rcpp**
(`nilhmm/src/rtiger.cpp`), removing the Julia backend that upstream RTIGER
\[Campos-Martin 2023\] requires through `JuliaCall`. The reimplementation is a
**prerequisite**, not a contribution: the paper's simulation-scale calibration and
benchmarking (a caller rerun across many parameter settings on 10^4-10^5-marker
panels) are only tractable on a laptop once the per-fit cost and the Julia runtime
dependency are gone. This note establishes two things, in order of importance:

1. **Correctness first.** The C++/Rcpp core returns the *same answer* as the Julia
   RTIGER, Viterbi-identical ancestry paths and fitted parameters agreeing to
   float-summation tolerance, from identical initialization on real data. **Result:
   0 mismatches at every size including the full 109,703-marker panel (329,109 of
   329,109 states), same iteration count, params to <=4e-6.**
2. **Then speed/memory.** Only after equivalence is established do we report the
   throughput and memory gains. **Result: the C++ core is near-linear (per-iter
   markers^0.98) vs the original's markers^1.69 at r=250; full-fit speed-up ~2,000x
   at the smallest size to ~12,000x at the full 110k panel, and ~6-7x less resident
   memory.**

::: {.callout-note title="Rigidity is collapsed, so r=250 is nearly free for the C++ core"}
RTIGER's rigid (Erlang-r) segment prior is implemented in the C++ core in
**collapsed** form, a windowed emission product plus an r-step transition offset,
not an explicit s x r dwell-state enumeration (verified in `rtiger.cpp`, a
bit-identical port of the Julia `rHMM_methods.jl`). Consequently the C++
per-iteration time and memory are essentially independent of r, whereas the upstream
original recomputes each length-r emission window at O(T s r). At the operating
r=250 that makes the windowed-product optimization a first-order win, on top of the
emission M-step regrouping and the streaming that removes the sample-count (N) memory
factor. See the paper supplement (`nilhmm-paper/supplement_optimization.tex`, Sec.
1) for the per-step complexity accounting.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Compute is separated from this note"}
The C++ benchmark is run by `scripts/bench_rtiger_r250.R`: it builds the shared
*Arabidopsis* Col x Ler panel, thins it by odd index to five sizes, fits the nilHMM
C++ core at each size (to convergence + a per-iteration delta trace), and compares
against the original-Julia oracle. The original at r=250 is run at all five sizes by
`scripts/rtiger_julia_conv_worker.jl` (per-iteration log + params/Viterbi dump). Peak
memory is measured on cleanly thinned inputs: `scripts/materialize_thinned_panel.R`
writes `thin_L{0..4}/`, then `scripts/bench_rtiger_cpp_memory_markers.R`
(C++, `RTIGER_RIG=250`) and `scripts/rtiger_julia_mem_worker.jl` (original) read only
that thinned set. Figures are written by `scripts/rtiger_r250_plot.R` to
`nilhmm-paper/figures/`; this note only loads and displays the CSVs and figures. The
C++ sweep runs in seconds; the original's full panel takes ~43 min.

Inputs staged from the fork (see `DATA.md`): the 109,703-locus shared-3 panel
(`data/rtiger_shared3_input/`), originally from BioProject PRJNA720439 / SRP313911.

Machine/toolchain for the timings is recorded per run in
`results/bench/rtiger_env.txt` (Apple M4, 10 cores, 24 GB, macOS 26.5; nilHMM
C++/Rcpp built native arm64 with Apple clang 17 at `-O2`; R 4.5.2; single-threaded;
original-Julia baseline on Julia 1.12.6, same hardware). The core's
forward/backward, Viterbi, and emission M-step are hand-written scalar C++
(`LinkingTo`: only Rcpp and RcppParallel; no Armadillo, Eigen, or external
BLAS/LAPACK), so the system linear-algebra library cannot affect these timings.
:::

```{r setup}
suppressMessages({
  library(data.table)
})
BENCH <- here::here("results/bench")
f <- function(x) file.path(BENCH, x)
```

## 1. Why reimplement in C++/Rcpp

Two properties of the upstream implementation motivate the port:

- **Runtime dependency.** Upstream RTIGER runs its time-critical core in Julia via
  `JuliaCall`; a matching Julia + package environment must be installed and loaded.
  For an R package intended to run many callers on many parameter settings, that
  cross-runtime dependency is both a friction and a reproducibility hazard.
- **Per-fit cost at density.** The paper works at 10^4-10^5 markers per line and
  reruns callers across calibration sweeps. The fit must be cheap enough to loop.

The C++/Rcpp core keeps the rigid-HMM arithmetic (rHMM EM + rigid Viterbi + segment
boundary postprocessing) but runs in-process with no external runtime.

## 2. Equivalence (correctness first)

The claim the port must earn: **same answer as Julia RTIGER**. Both cores are run to
full convergence from identical deterministic initialization at r=250, at all five
sizes including the full 109,703-marker panel, and outputs compared position-by-position.

```{r equiv-table}
eq <- fread(f("rtiger_equiv_r250.csv"))
knitr::kable(
  eq,
  col.names = c(
    "level", "markers/sample", "iters (C++)", "iters (Julia)",
    "Viterbi total", "mismatches", "param max-diff"
  ),
  caption = "nilHMM C++/Rcpp vs upstream-original Julia RTIGER at r=250, run to convergence from the identical deterministic init, at all five sizes including the full 109,703-marker panel: same EM iteration count, Viterbi-identical ancestry paths (0 mismatches), parameters agreeing to float-summation tolerance."
)
```

The two cores descend the identical path to convergence at the full panel (right);
the left panel is the speed contrast:

![Full panel (109,703 markers/sample), r=250, both cores run to convergence (4 iterations each). Left: per-iteration wall time (original Julia ~638 s/iter vs nilHMM C++/Rcpp ~0.05 s/iter). Right: each iteration's EM parameter-convergence delta (max |Delta(alpha,beta)|, RTIGER's convergence criterion, not a log-likelihood change), C++ against original Julia on a log-log 1:1 line, early (yellow) to late (purple).](../nilhmm-paper/figures/rtiger_equivalence_r250.png)

## 3. Throughput, scaling with markers

Shared-panel head-to-head on the five thinned sizes
(6,857 -> 13,713 -> 27,426 -> 54,852 -> 109,703), all measured for both cores. The
**figure** plots *per-iteration* wall time, whose log-log slope is the empirical
complexity exponent: at r=250 the original is markers^1.69, the C++/Rcpp core
markers^0.98 (linear). The **table** reports *full-fit* wall time to convergence and
the resulting speed-up.

```{r scaling-table}
sc <- fread(f("rtiger_marker_scaling_r250.csv"))
w <- dcast(sc, mps ~ core, value.var = "total")[order(-mps)]
w[, speedup := round(orig / cpp)]
knitr::kable(
  w[, .(
    `markers/sample` = mps, `C++/Rcpp (s)` = round(cpp, 3),
    `orig Julia (s)` = round(orig), `speed-up` = speedup
  )],
  caption = "Full-fit wall time to convergence at r=250 (3 samples, eps=0.01), all five sizes measured; the speed-up grows with problem size because the C++ core is near-linear."
)
```

![RTIGER scaling on the shared panel at r=250, original array-retaining Julia vs nilHMM C++/Rcpp. Points are measurements at all five sizes (both cores); dashed lines the power-law fits. Left: per-iteration wall time (original ~markers^1.69, C++/Rcpp ~markers^0.98 linear). Right: peak resident memory on cleanly thinned inputs; the C++ core streams sufficient statistics and stays ~6-7x lower.](../nilhmm-paper/figures/rtiger_marker_scaling_r250.png)

## 4. Memory

The C++ E-step folds each chain into fixed-size pooled sufficient statistics (the
s x s transition counts, the start counts, and a map of gamma-weighted counts over
the distinct (k, n) read-count pairs) and discards each chain's per-position
zeta/gamma/alpha/beta arrays immediately. Peak scratch is therefore one chain's
worth, O(T_max s^2), independent of the sample count N, whereas the array-retaining
original holds all chains at once, O(N T s^2). Because the rigidity is collapsed, the
C++ memory is also essentially independent of r.

```{r mem-table}
cm <- fread(f("rtiger_memory_markers_r250.csv"))
jm <- fread(f("rtiger_julia_memory_markers_r250.csv"))
setnames(cm, "peak_rss_mib", "C++/Rcpp (MiB)")
setnames(jm, "peak_rss_mib", "orig Julia (MiB)")
mm <- merge(cm, jm, by = "markers", all = TRUE)[order(-markers)]
mm[, ratio := round(`orig Julia (MiB)` / `C++/Rcpp (MiB)`, 1)]
knitr::kable(mm,
  col.names = c("markers/sample", "C++/Rcpp (MiB)", "orig Julia (MiB)", "ratio"),
  caption = "Peak resident memory at r=250 (3 samples), C++ vs original Julia, all five sizes measured on cleanly thinned inputs (each process loads only its own marker set). Both scale with marker count (per-chain arrays are O(T)); the ~6-7x separation is the original's per-chain array retention, and it widens further on the sample axis."
)
```

On cleanly thinned inputs both cores scale with marker count (the per-chain arrays
are O(T)); the ~6-7x separation is the original's per-chain array retention. The
asymptotically meaningful axis is the sample count N, on which the streamed C++
scratch does not grow while the original's does.

## 5. Reproducing

`scripts/bench_rtiger_r250.R` builds the shared panel from the staged Col x Ler
inputs, runs the C++ core at each size (to convergence + delta trace), compares
against the original-Julia oracle at all five sizes, and writes the CSVs.
`scripts/rtiger_julia_conv_worker.jl <orig_base> <level> 250` produces the original
runs; `scripts/rtiger_r250_plot.R` writes the figures. Only the built nilHMM C++ core
and (for the small original sizes) Julia are needed.
